title: The Primary and Secondary Corporate Credit Facilities

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This Federal Reserve Bank of New York research paper analyzes the design, operations, and impact of the Primary Market Corporate Credit Facility (PMCCF) and Secondary Market Corporate Credit Facility (SMCCF), the Fed's first-ever interventions in corporate bond markets launched in March 2020 during the COVID-19 pandemic. The authors show that while the PMCCF never saw any usage, the SMCCF purchased $14 billion in bonds and ETFs before ceasing purchases on December 31, 2020, with the announcement itself having a dramatic stabilizing effect on liquidity and pricing in secondary markets and reigniting primary market issuance.
key_points: In March 2020, corporate bond yield spreads widened approximately 250 bps for investment-grade and 550 bps for high-yield bonds during the market seizure
Corporate bond mutual funds and ETFs experienced nearly $200 billion in outflows during the last two weeks of March 2020
SMCCF commenced ETF purchases on May 12, 2020 and bond purchases on June 16, 2020, making total purchases of $14 billion before ceasing on December 31, 2020
PMCCF became operational on June 29, 2020 but no qualified borrower ever expressed need to use the facility
New issue concession reached 50+ bps above secondary market yields (approximately double the usual level) before Fed intervention
High-yield corporate bond issuance halted almost entirely between March 4 and April 16, 2020
why_it_matters: This paper documents how Federal Reserve market intervention can restore market functioning through announcement effects alone. The finding that the mere announcement of the CCFs stabilized markets—without requiring actual facility usage—has significant implications for understanding the power of credible central bank backstops in resolving market dislocations. The distinction between program availability and actual usage is crucial for assessing the efficiency of emergency liquidity facilities.
market_structure_context: The paper documents a severe market structure crisis in March 2020 where bid-ask spreads deteriorated to levels not seen since the global financial crisis. The SMCCF operated as a buyer of last resort in corporate bond ETFs and individual bonds, purchasing $14 billion total. The secondary market for corporate bonds is historically over-the-counter with dealer intermediation. The PMCCF was designed to purchase new bond issuances directly from issuers but never saw usage, suggesting the announcement backstop alone was sufficient to restore primary market functioning. The SMCCF wind-down portfolio was announced June 2, 2021 and completed by August 31, 2021.
important_numbers: $200 billion in outflows from corporate bond funds (late March 2020)
250 bps IG spread widening / 550 bps HY spread widening
SMCCF ETF purchases began May 12, 2020; bond purchases June 16, 2020; ceased December 31, 2020
notes: Source is a high-quality Federal Reserve Bank of New York Economic Policy Review working paper. Historical record (2022 publication analyzing 2020 events). Authors note views are their own and do not necessarily reflect FRBNY or Fed System positions. Paper is analytically rigorous and well-sourced with internal Fed data. The SMCCF wind-down was completed August 31, 2021. The key insight is the demonstration of announcement effect power in emergency market interventions.
